title: Archon + Jira: Drag a Ticket, Get a Pull Request (Live Build)
summary: Cole Medin demonstrates building a Jira adapter for Archon that automatically triggers AI-driven code fixes from tickets. When a Jira ticket arrives, Archon analyzes it, generates a fix, opens a pull request, and posts the result back to the ticket. Each ticket gets a dedicated conversation context within Archon.
writeup: This live stream builds a Jira community adapter for Archon, an open-source agent orchestration framework, so that developers can hold per-ticket conversations with a coding agent directly inside Jira, mirroring how Archon already handles parallel conversations via Slack threads. The host uses a 12-step Archon workflow called PIV System Evolution, which takes a GitHub issue as input and produces a pull request as output, to build the adapter itself. The broader argument is that Jira's near-universal adoption in enterprise development makes it the highest-leverage integration surface, and that mapping one agent conversation per ticket is more useful than fully automated ticket resolution. A concrete model-efficiency finding from prior benchmarking drives the run: Opus 4.8 handles only the planning step, and Sonnet handles everything else.
key_points: Archon supports multiple adapter types (web UI, CLI, Slack, GitHub, Telegram, Discord), each mapping a platform's thread or channel primitive to a dedicated Archon conversation; the Jira adapter follows the same pattern by mapping individual Jira tickets to conversations rather than polling the board automatically.

The adapter taxonomy splits into maintainer-supported adapters and community adapters, the latter contributed and self-maintained by outside developers; Jira is being introduced as a community adapter, with Gitea and GitLab as prior examples in that category.

The PIV (Plan, Implement, Validate) System Evolution workflow is a 12-step Archon workflow that loads codebase context, generates a plan from a GitHub issue, executes implementation with validation steps (type checking, linting, manual and code review), then produces a pull request, with human-approval gates at the plan and implementation stages to keep the engineer in the loop.

Benchmarking from a prior session showed that using Opus 4.8 exclusively for the planning step and Sonnet for all remaining steps (implementation, validation, system evolution) produces results close to all-Opus quality at significantly lower token cost, making this the adopted strategy for the live build.

The intended Jira interaction model is conversational rather than fire-and-forget: a developer comments inside a ticket and Archon replies in-thread, running workflows on demand, with ticket movement (column transitions on the Kanban board) handled by a Jira skill inside those workflows rather than by the adapter itself.

Model Selection: Opus for Planning, Sonnet for Execution
